label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out the answer to this question. The question is asking why small pieces of space junk can threaten satellites. The options are A, B, C, D. Let me read through the provided text again to find the relevant information. The text mentions that even bits of space junk as small as dots of paint pose a threat because this debris orbits the Earth at speeds up to 28,200 kilometers per hour. The high speed explains why tiny paint dots have damaged the windshields of space shuttles so badly that they needed to be replaced. So the key points here are the size of the debris (small) and their speed (very high). The reason they are dangerous is because of their high velocity, which gives them a lot of kinetic energy. Even small objects moving at such speeds can cause significant damage upon impact. Looking at the options: A) They travel at a very high speed. This matches with what the text says. The text directly states that the high speed is why small pieces are dangerous. B) They are difficult to track by satellites. The text doesn't mention tracking as an issue. The problem is their speed and the damage they can do, not necessarily tracking them. C) They can blow up into millions of pieces. The text doesn't say that small pieces explode or blow up. It's about the existing debris causing damage. D) They are as soft as a ball in the air. That doesn't make sense. The text actually says the opposite—paint dots caused damage, implying they are hard or solid enough to do harm. A soft object wouldn't cause such damage. So the correct answer should be A. The high speed is the reason small pieces threaten satellites.
